findings.summary: All 8 case handler classes use an identical hasattr/callable/getattr triple-check dispatch pattern that is verbose boilerplate repeated 19 times across the cases/ directory
evidence: Every process_update method opens with: `if hasattr(model, '_update_X') and callable(getattr(model, '_update_X')): return model._update_X(data)`
Grep count shows 19 occurrences of this pattern across 8 case files

The calculate_free_energy methods in nominative, accusative, and dative each repeat an identical structure: set default_fe, check two hasattr guards, getattr, try array norm, except broad tuple, return default
findings.suggestion: Extract a dispatch helper such as `_delegate(model, method_name, data)` in a shared base or mixin, and a `_calc_array_fe(a, b, default)` utility. This would eliminate the repeated 5-line dispatch blocks and 10-line free-energy patterns across all 8 files.

findings.summary: Nearly every method across case handlers has identical one-line Args/Returns docstring sections that state the obvious without adding information
evidence: nominative.py apply(): Args section says `model: The model to configure for nominative case` and Returns says `The configured model` — both restate the type hints already in the signature
calculate_free_energy docstrings say `Returns: The calculated free energy` across all 8 case files with no additional domain context

Comment blocks like `# Standard nominative behavior: goal-directed action` and `# Handle different types of data receipt` describe the code structure without explaining the cognitive-science rationale
findings.suggestion: Trim Args/Returns sections to non-obvious parameters only. Replace structural comments with domain-level reasoning (e.g., why precision differs between cases).

findings.summary: src/cases/__init__.py mixes relative imports for case handlers with an absolute `from src.core.model import Case` import, creating an inconsistent import style within a single __init__ file
evidence: Lines 17-26: all case handler imports use relative style `from .nominative import NominativeCase` etc.
Line 29: `from src.core.model import Case` breaks the relative convention inside the same file
The codebase elsewhere also mixes absolute `from src.X` imports (41 files, 86 occurrences) with relative imports, indicating no enforced convention
findings.suggestion: Change line 29 in src/cases/__init__.py to `from ..core.model import Case` to be consistent with the other imports in that file. Consider adding an isort or flake8 rule to enforce relative-within-package imports.

findings.summary: Multiple test files define local stub/compat classes (_BareModel, _InsectCompat, _PlainModel) to work around API surface limitations, suggesting the real classes are hard to instantiate for unit testing
evidence: test_case_default_paths.py defines `_BareModel` (a 9-attribute manual stub) because `Model` carries unwanted case dispatch side-effects
test_case_handlers.py defines `_PlainModel` inline within a test method with the comment: 'instead of deleting the method from the Model class (which corrupts all subsequent Model instances)'
test_behavior_visualizer.py defines `_InsectCompat` with `__getattr__` delegation and overridden properties solely to reconcile attribute name mismatches with visualization code
findings.suggestion: Expose a minimal constructor path or a test-friendly factory on Model/InsectModel that suppresses case dispatch side-effects. Where behavior_visualizer expects `current_case` and `behavioral_state` but the model exposes `case` and `_behavioral_state`, align the public API rather than papering over it in tests.
